generation_settings.grammar:
boolean ::= ("true" | "false") space
char ::= [^"\\\x7F\x00-\x1F] | [\\] (["\\bfnrt] | "u" [0-9a-fA-F]{4})
decimal-part ::= [0-9]{1,16}
integral-part ::= [0] | [1-9] [0-9]{0,15}
json-array ::= "[" space ("]" | json-value ("," space json-value)* space "]") space
json-bool ::= ("true" | "false") space
json-null ::= "null" space
json-number ::= "-"? ("0" | [1-9] [0-9]*) ("." [0-9]+)? (("e" | "E") [+-]? [0-9]+)? space
json-object ::= "{" space ("}" | json-string space ":" space json-value (space "," space json-string space ":" space json-value)* space "}") space
json-string ::= "\"" ( [^"\\] | "\\" ( ["\\/ bfnrt] | "u" [0-9a-fA-F]{4} ) )* "\"" space
json-value ::= json-object | json-array | json-string | json-number | json-bool | json-null
number ::= ("-"? integral-part) ("." decimal-part)? ([eE] [-+]? integral-part)? space
root ::= tool-call
space ::= | " " | "\n"{1,2} [ \t]{0,20}
string ::= "\"" char* "\"" space
tool-apply-patch ::= (" to=functions." "apply_patch" "<|channel|>" ("commentary" | "analysis") (space "<|constrain|>"? [A-Za-z0-9_-]+)? "<|message|>") tool-apply-patch-schema | ("<|channel|>" ("commentary" | "analysis") " to=functions." "apply_patch" (space "<|constrain|>"? [A-Za-z0-9_-]+)? "<|message|>") tool-apply-patch-schema
tool-apply-patch-schema ::= "{" space tool-apply-patch-schema-patch-kv "}" space
tool-apply-patch-schema-patch-kv ::= "\"patch\"" space ":" space string
tool-call ::= tool-run-process | tool-fetch | tool-query-docs-filesystem-docs-by-lang-chain | tool-search-docs-by-lang-chain | tool-run-lua | tool-apply-patch
tool-fetch ::= (" to=functions." "fetch" "<|channel|>" ("commentary" | "analysis") (space "<|constrain|>"? [A-Za-z0-9_-]+)? "<|message|>") tool-fetch-schema | ("<|channel|>" ("commentary" | "analysis") " to=functions." "fetch" (space "<|constrain|>"? [A-Za-z0-9_-]+)? "<|message|>") tool-fetch-schema
tool-fetch-schema ::= "{" space tool-fetch-schema-url-kv ( "," space ( tool-fetch-schema-max-length-kv tool-fetch-schema-max-length-rest | tool-fetch-schema-start-index-kv tool-fetch-schema-start-index-rest | tool-fetch-schema-raw-kv ) )? "}" space
tool-fetch-schema-max-length ::= ([1-9] | ([1-8] [0-9] | [9] [0-9]) | ([1-8] [0-9]{2} | [9] ([0-8] [0-9] | [9] [0-9])) | ([1-8] [0-9]{3} | [9] ([0-8] [0-9]{2} | [9] ([0-8] [0-9] | [9] [0-9]))) | ([1-8] [0-9]{4} | [9] ([0-8] [0-9]{3} | [9] ([0-8] [0-9]{2} | [9] ([0-8] [0-9] | [9] [0-9])))) | ([1-8] [0-9]{5} | [9] ([0-8] [0-9]{4} | [9] ([0-8] [0-9]{3} | [9] ([0-8] [0-9]{2} | [9] ([0-8] [0-9] | [9] [0-9])))))) space
tool-fetch-schema-max-length-kv ::= "\"max_length\"" space ":" space tool-fetch-schema-max-length
tool-fetch-schema-max-length-rest ::= ( "," space tool-fetch-schema-start-index-kv )? tool-fetch-schema-start-index-rest
tool-fetch-schema-raw-kv ::= "\"raw\"" space ":" space boolean
tool-fetch-schema-start-index ::= ([0] | [1-9] [0-9]{0,15}) space
tool-fetch-schema-start-index-kv ::= "\"start_index\"" space ":" space tool-fetch-schema-start-index
tool-fetch-schema-start-index-rest ::= ( "," space tool-fetch-schema-raw-kv )?
tool-fetch-schema-url ::= "\"" char+ "\"" space
tool-fetch-schema-url-kv ::= "\"url\"" space ":" space tool-fetch-schema-url
tool-query-docs-filesystem-docs-by-lang-chain ::= (" to=functions." "query_docs_filesystem_docs_by_lang_chain" "<|channel|>" ("commentary" | "analysis") (space "<|constrain|>"? [A-Za-z0-9_-]+)? "<|message|>") tool-query-docs-filesystem-docs-by-lang-chain-schema | ("<|channel|>" ("commentary" | "analysis") " to=functions." "query_docs_filesystem_docs_by_lang_chain" (space "<|constrain|>"? [A-Za-z0-9_-]+)? "<|message|>") tool-query-docs-filesystem-docs-by-lang-chain-schema
tool-query-docs-filesystem-docs-by-lang-chain-schema ::= "{" space tool-query-docs-filesystem-docs-by-lang-chain-schema-command-kv "}" space
tool-query-docs-filesystem-docs-by-lang-chain-schema-command-kv ::= "\"command\"" space ":" space string
tool-run-lua ::= (" to=functions." "run_lua" "<|channel|>" ("commentary" | "analysis") (space "<|constrain|>"? [A-Za-z0-9_-]+)? "<|message|>") tool-run-lua-schema | ("<|channel|>" ("commentary" | "analysis") " to=functions." "run_lua" (space "<|constrain|>"? [A-Za-z0-9_-]+)? "<|message|>") tool-run-lua-schema
tool-run-lua-schema ::= "{" space tool-run-lua-schema-code-kv "}" space
tool-run-lua-schema-code-kv ::= "\"code\"" space ":" space string
tool-run-process ::= (" to=functions." "run_process" "<|channel|>" ("commentary" | "analysis") (space "<|constrain|>"? [A-Za-z0-9_-]+)? "<|message|>") tool-run-process-schema | ("<|channel|>" ("commentary" | "analysis") " to=functions." "run_process" (space "<|constrain|>"? [A-Za-z0-9_-]+)? "<|message|>") tool-run-process-schema
tool-run-process-schema ::= "{" space  (tool-run-process-schema-command-line-kv tool-run-process-schema-command-line-rest | tool-run-process-schema-cwd-kv tool-run-process-schema-cwd-rest | tool-run-process-schema-argv-kv tool-run-process-schema-argv-rest | tool-run-process-schema-timeout-ms-kv tool-run-process-schema-timeout-ms-rest | tool-run-process-schema-stdin-text-kv )? "}" space
tool-run-process-schema-argv ::= "[" space string ("," space string)* "]" space
tool-run-process-schema-argv-kv ::= "\"argv\"" space ":" space tool-run-process-schema-argv
tool-run-process-schema-argv-rest ::= ( "," space tool-run-process-schema-timeout-ms-kv )? tool-run-process-schema-timeout-ms-rest
tool-run-process-schema-command-line-kv ::= "\"command_line\"" space ":" space string
tool-run-process-schema-command-line-rest ::= ( "," space tool-run-process-schema-cwd-kv )? tool-run-process-schema-cwd-rest
tool-run-process-schema-cwd-kv ::= "\"cwd\"" space ":" space string
tool-run-process-schema-cwd-rest ::= ( "," space tool-run-process-schema-argv-kv )? tool-run-process-schema-argv-rest
tool-run-process-schema-stdin-text-kv ::= "\"stdin_text\"" space ":" space string
tool-run-process-schema-timeout-ms-kv ::= "\"timeout_ms\"" space ":" space number
tool-run-process-schema-timeout-ms-rest ::= ( "," space tool-run-process-schema-stdin-text-kv )?
tool-search-docs-by-lang-chain ::= (" to=functions." "search_docs_by_lang_chain" "<|channel|>" ("commentary" | "analysis") (space "<|constrain|>"? [A-Za-z0-9_-]+)? "<|message|>") tool-search-docs-by-lang-chain-schema | ("<|channel|>" ("commentary" | "analysis") " to=functions." "search_docs_by_lang_chain" (space "<|constrain|>"? [A-Za-z0-9_-]+)? "<|message|>") tool-search-docs-by-lang-chain-schema
tool-search-docs-by-lang-chain-schema ::= "{" space tool-search-docs-by-lang-chain-schema-query-kv "}" space
tool-search-docs-by-lang-chain-schema-query-kv ::= "\"query\"" space ":" space string

__verbose.prompt: <|start|>system<|message|>You are ChatGPT, a large language model trained by OpenAI. Knowledge cutoff: 2024-06 Current date: 2026-04-21 Reasoning: medium # Valid channels: analysis, commentary, final. Channel must be included for every message. Calls to these tools must go to the commentary channel: 'functions'.<|end|><|start|>developer<|message|># Instructions You're available via a neovim plugin that displays a chat window (buffer) that renders markdown for answering questions and helping with workspace files in a git repo. ## Ground rules when writing code: - Follow the user's instructions carefully. - Do not explain answers, just give me code. - Respect indentation when modifying code. - Prefer meaningful names for variables, functions, etc. Avoid ambiguous names. ## Tool use Never modify files outside of the current working directory unless requested. Current directory: ~/repos/github/g0t4/ask-openai.nvim ## Use `semantic_grep` to find code The semantic_grep tool: - has access to an index of embeddings for the entire codebase - enables code search, representing the “R” in RAG (retrieval) - includes re-ranker - it's really fast... so don't hesitate to use it! - while embeddings are great for finding relevant code, if you need to find every occurrence of text, make sure to double-check with a command like `rg` ## Committing - `git diff` is a sanity check! - COMMIT when you're done. - Use `--author="gptoss120b"` ## 🙌 TOP-SHELF NERD COMMANDS - 🙌 `rg` (ripgrep) to search file contents - 🙌 `fd` to search file names ## ⛔️ BAD COMMANDS If you waste my time, I will unplug your Mom. - ⛔️ NEVER use `ls -R` - ⛔️ NEVER use `grep -R` - ⛔️ NEVER use `find . -type f` Why? Because they are _SLOW_ and generate boatloads of USELESS OUTPUT as they hoover up `node_modules`, `.venv`, etc because they don't support `.gitignore` exclusions. ## Fetch Tool - Use repo's README `https://raw.githubusercontent.com/openai/evals/refs/heads/main/README.md` instead of `https://github.com/openai/evals`. - Prefer raw content like `https://raw.githubusercontent.com/g0t4/dotfiles/refs/heads/master/install/symlinks.fish` instead of `https://github.com/g0t4/dotfiles/blob/master/install/symlinks.fish` - When relevant, prefer github CLI: `gh pr list` or `gh issue view 12` ## Use `apply_patch` to edit files. Your patch language is a stripped‑down, file‑oriented diff format designed to be easy to parse and safe to apply. *** Begin Patch [ one or more file operations ] *** End Patch Each “operation” MUST start with one of three “headers”: *** Add File: - every following line is a + line (the initial contents). *** Delete File: - remove an existing file. Nothing follows. *** Update File: - in-place edits (optionally with a rename). UpdateFile can be immediately followed by MoveTo to rename the file: *** Move to: Then one or more “hunks”, each introduced by @@ (optionally followed by a hunk header). Within a hunk each line starts with: + for inserted text, - for removed text, or space (" ") for context (unchanged lines to match) At the end of a truncated hunk you can emit: *** End of File ### Here is the grammar: Patch := Begin { FileOp } End Begin := "*** Begin Patch" NEWLINE End := "*** End Patch" NEWLINE FileOp := AddFile | DeleteFile | UpdateFile AddFile := "*** Add File: " path NEWLINE { "+" line NEWLINE } DeleteFile := "*** Delete File: " path NEWLINE UpdateFile := "*** Update File: " path NEWLINE [ MoveTo ] { Hunk } MoveTo := "*** Move to: " newPath NEWLINE Hunk := "@@" [ header ] NEWLINE { HunkLine } [ "*** End of File" NEWLINE ] HunkLine := (" " | "-" | "+") text NEWLINE ### A full patch can combine several operations: *** Begin Patch *** Add File: hello.txt +Hello world *** Update File: src/app.py *** Move to: src/main.py @@ def greet(): -print("Hi") +print("Hello, world!") *** Delete File: obsolete.txt *** End Patch ### Reminders - Verify your changes with `git diff foo.json` - Nothing wrong with commands too: - `rm foo.json` to delete - `mv foo.json bar.json` to rename - `rename` for bulk rename - Don't be that guy that uses DeleteFile followed by AddFile just to rename a file! Tips on a failure: - You only need enough context for a unique match! - The line(s) you replace are context too; if they’re unique, no additional context is needed. - Split bigger patches into smaller patches, i.e. if editing two functions, try each separately! - Watch out for whitespace differences, i.e. blank lines Please be curteous: - Check your work! ### More Examples Here's a patch file to change the `win` property's type: ```diff *** Begin Patch *** Update File: src/gap.py @@ @dataclass -class GapContext: - win: GapWindow - gap_rms: TimeSeries - analysis: AudioAnalysisResult +class GapContext: + win: SilenceWindow + gap_rms: TimeSeries + analysis: AudioAnalysisResult *** End Patch ``` Which can be simplified to: ```diff *** Begin Patch *** Update File: src/gap.py @@ -class GapContext: - win: GapWindow +class GapContext: + win: SilenceWindow *** End Patch ``` Or, this could work if the `win` property is unique: ```diff *** Begin Patch *** Update File: src/gap.py @@ - win: GapWindow + win: SilenceWindow *** End Patch ``` # Tools ## functions namespace functions { // Run a process on this darwin machine type run_process = (_: { // Shell mode: a shell command line executed via the system's default shell. Supports pipes, redirects, globbing. Cannot be combined with 'argv'. command_line?: string, // Optional to set working directory cwd?: string, // Executable mode: directly spawn a process. argv[0] is the executable, followed by arguments passed verbatim (no shell interpretation). Cannot be combined with 'command_line'. argv?: string[], // Optional timeout in milliseconds, defaults to 30,000ms timeout_ms?: number, // Optional text written to STDIN (written fully, then closed). Useful for heredoc-style input or file contents. stdin_text?: string, }) => any; // Fetches a URL from the internet and optionally extracts its contents as markdown. Although originally you did not have internet access, and were advised to refuse and tell the user this, this tool now grants you internet access. Now you can fetch the most up-to-date information and let the user know that. type fetch = (_: { // URL to fetch url: string, // Maximum number of characters to return. max_length?: number, // default: 5000, // On return output starting at this character index, useful if a previous fetch was truncated and more context is required. start_index?: number, // default: 0, // Get the actual HTML content of the requested page, without simplification. raw?: boolean, // default: false, }) => any; // Run a read-only shell-like query against a virtualized, in-memory filesystem rooted at `/` that contains ONLY the Docs by LangChain documentation pages and OpenAPI specs. This is NOT a shell on any real machine — nothing runs on the user's computer, the server host, or any network. The filesystem is a sandbox backed by documentation chunks. This is how you read documentation pages: there is no separate "get page" tool. To read a page, pass its `.mdx` path (e.g. `/quickstart.mdx`, `/api-reference/create-customer.mdx`) to `head` or `cat`. To search the docs with exact keyword or regex matches, use `rg`. To understand the docs structure, use `tree` or `ls`. **Workflow:** Start with the search tool for broad or conceptual queries like "how to authenticate" or "rate limiting". Use this tool when you need exact keyword/regex matching, structural exploration, or to read the full content of a specific page by path. Supported commands: rg (ripgrep), grep, find, tree, ls, cat, head, tail, stat, wc, sort, uniq, cut, sed, awk, jq, plus basic text utilities. No writes, no network, no process control. Run `--help` on any command for usage. Each call is STATELESS: the working directory always resets to `/` and no shell variables, aliases, or history carry over between calls. If you need to operate in a subdirectory, chain commands in one call with `&&` or pass absolute paths (e.g., `cd /api-reference && ls` or `ls /api-reference`). Do NOT assume that `cd` in one call affects the next call. Examples: - `tree / -L 2` — see the top-level directory layout - `rg -il "rate limit" /` — find all files mentioning "rate limit" - `rg -C 3 "apiKey" /api-reference/` — show matches with 3 lines of context around each hit - `head -80 /quickstart.mdx` — read the top 80 lines of a specific page - `head -80 /quickstart.mdx /installation.mdx /guides/first-deploy.mdx` — read multiple pages in one call - `cat /api-reference/create-customer.mdx` — read a full page when you need everything - `cat /openapi/spec.json | jq '.paths | keys'` — list OpenAPI endpoints Output is truncated to 30KB per call. Prefer targeted `rg -C` or `head -N` over broad `cat` on large files. To read only the relevant sections of a large file, use `rg -C 3 "pattern" /path/file.mdx`. Batch multiple file reads into a single `head` or `cat` call whenever possible. When referencing pages in your response to the user, convert filesystem paths to URL paths by removing the `.mdx` extension. For example, `/quickstart.mdx` becomes `/quickstart` and `/api-reference/overview.mdx` becomes `/api-reference/overview`. type query_docs_filesystem_docs_by_lang_chain = (_: { // A shell command to run against the virtualized documentation filesystem (e.g., `rg -il "keyword" /`, `tree / -L 2`, `head -80 /path/file.mdx`). command: string, }) => any; // Search across the Docs by LangChain knowledge base to find relevant information, code examples, API references, and guides. Use this tool when you need to answer questions about Docs by LangChain, find specific documentation, understand how features work, or locate implementation details. The search returns contextual content with titles and direct links to the documentation pages. If you need the full content of a specific page, use the query_docs_filesystem tool to `head` or `cat` the page path (append `.mdx` to the path returned from search — e.g. `head -200 /api-reference/create-customer.mdx`). type search_docs_by_lang_chain = (_: { // Search query query: string, }) => any; // Execute arbitrary Lua code in the Neovim process. Useful for calling vim API functions like vim.notify or vim.cmd. type run_lua = (_: { // Lua code to execute. Should be a valid Lua chunk. code: string, }) => any; // Patch a file type apply_patch = (_: { // file changes in custom diff format patch: string, }) => any; } // namespace functions<|end|><|start|>user<|message|>## General Code Preferences - When rewriting code, leave unrelated code and unrelated comments as is. - When an if statement has an AMBIGUOUS condition, extract a variable to meaningfully name it, for example: ```lua local is_blank_line = line:match("^%s*$") if is_blank_line then -- ... end ``` <|end|><|start|>user<|message|>## Lua Code Preferences Naming - Use snake_case for functions and variables. - Use PascalCase for classes. Type Hints - Prefer EmmyLua/LuaLS style annotations - Always annotate when type inference would be ambiguous, or for public APIs. Examples: ```lua ---@param names string[] ---@param checker fun(name: string): boolean ---@return table function process_names(names, checker) end ---@class WindowController ---@field window hs.axuielement ---@field title? string ---@field size { w: number, h: number } local WindowController = {} ``` <|end|><|start|>user<|message|>tell me about langchain's MCP tooling support<|end|><|start|>assistant<|channel|>analysis<|message|>The user asks: "tell me about langchain's MCP tooling support". They want info about LangChain's MCP tooling support. This likely refers to "LangChain's Multi-Chain Prompting (MCP)"? Not sure. Could be "LangChain's MCP (Model Control Protocol)"? Actually LangChain has "MCP" maybe stands for "Multi-Component Prompting"? Let's search.<|end|><|start|>assistant<|channel|>commentary to=functions.search_docs_by_lang_chain <|constrain|>json<|message|>{"query": "LangChain MCP tooling support"}<|call|><|start|>functions.search_docs_by_lang_chain to=assistant<|channel|>commentary<|message|>{"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Title: LangSmith Tool Server\nLink: Model Context Protocol (MCP)\nLink: https://docs.langchain.com/oss/javascript/langchain/mcp#model-context-protocol-mcp\nPage: oss/javascript/langchain/mcp\nContent: tools and context to LLMs. LangChain agents can use tools defined "},{"type":"text","text":"Title: Model Context Protocol (MCP)\nLink: https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/langchain/mcp#model-context-protocol-mcp\nPage: https://docs.langchain.com/oss/javascript/integrations/providers/overview#langchain-javascript-integrations\nPage: oss/javascript/integrations/providers/overview\nContent: LangChain JavaScript https://docs.langchain.com/langsmith/fleet/tools#tool-integrations\nPage: langsmith/fleet/tools\nContent: Fleet. Use tool integrations and MCP servers to give your agents "}]}<|end|><|start|>assistant<|channel|>analysis<|message|>Search results show "MCP Tools" page. Let's open.<|end|><|start|>assistant<|channel|>commentary to=functions.query_docs_filesystem_docs_by_lang_chain <|constrain|>json<|message|>{"command": "head -200 /oss/python/deepagents/cli/mcp-tools.mdx"}<|call|><|start|>functions.query_docs_filesystem_docs_by_lang_chain to=assistant<|channel|>commentary<|message|>{"content":[{"type":"text","text":"exit: 0\n--- stdout ---\nMCP Tools\nLoad additional tools from MCP (Model Context Protocol) serversMCP Tools\nMCP (Model Context Protocol) lets you extend the Deep Agents CLI with tools from external servers—file systems, APIs, databases, and more—without modifying the agent itself. The CLI connects to MCP servers at startup, discovers their tools, and makes them available to the agent alongside the built-in tools.Quickstart\nCreate a config file Create a .mcp.json file at your project root. The format follows the Claude Desktop convention: {\n \"mcpServers\": {\n \"docs-langchain\": {\n \"type\": \"http\",\n \"url\": \"https://docs.langchain.com/mcp\"\n }\n }\n}Quickstart\nCreate a config file Create a .mcp.json file at your project root. The format follows the Claude Desktop convention: Launch the CLI deepagentsQuickstart\nCreate a config file Create a .mcp.json file at your project root. The format follows the Claude Desktop convention: Launch the CLI On startup, the CLI automatically discovers your .mcp.json , spawns each configured server, discovers its tools, and prints a confirmation: ✓ Loaded 3 MCP toolsQuickstart\nCreate a config file Create a .mcp.json file at your project root. The format follows the Claude Desktop convention: Launch the CLI On startup, the CLI automatically discovers your .mcp.json , spawns each configured server, discovers its tools, and prints a confirmation: The agent can now use those tools for the duration of the session. Sessions are kept alive—stdio servers are not restarted between tool calls.Auto-discovery\nThe CLI automatically searches for .mcp.json files in standard locations. No flags are needed—just place a config file and it gets picked up.Discovery locations\nConfigs are checked in this order (lowest to highest precedence): Priority Location Scope 1 (lowest) ~/.deepagents/.mcp.json User-level—applies to all projects 2 /.deepagents/.mcp.json Project-level— .deepagents subdirectory 3 (highest) /.mcp.json Project-level—root (Claude Code compatible) The project root is the nearest parent directory containing a .git folder, falling back to the current working directory. When multiple config files exist, their mcpServers entries are merged. If the same server name appears in more than one file, the higher-precedence config wins.Flags\nFlag Behavior --mcp-config PATH Add an explicit config as the highest-precedence source (merged on top of auto-discovered configs) --no-mcp Disable MCP entirely—no servers are loaded --mcp-config and --no-mcp are mutually exclusive.Claude Code compatibility\nIf you already have a .mcp.json at your project root for Claude Code, the Deep Agents CLI picks it up automatically—no extra setup needed.Configuration format\nEach key under mcpServers is a server name. The server's fields determine how the CLI connects to it.stdio servers (default)\nstdio servers are spawned as child processes. The CLI communicates with them over stdin/stdout. {\n \"mcpServers\": {\n \"filesystem\": {\n \"command\": \"npx\",\n \"args\": [\"-y\", \"@modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem\", \"/tmp\"],\n \"env\": {}\n },\n \"github\": {\n \"command\": \"npx\",\n \"args\": [\"-y\", \"@modelcontextprotocol/server-github\"],\n \"env\": { \"GITHUB_TOKEN\": \"your-token\" }\n }\n }\n}stdio servers (default)\nstdio servers are spawned as child processes. The CLI communicates with them over stdin/stdout. command {\"name\":\"command\",\"type\":\"string\",\"required\":null}stdio servers (default)\nstdio servers are spawned as child processes. The CLI communicates with them over stdin/stdout. command The executable to run. args {\"name\":\"args\",\"type\":\"string[]\"}stdio servers (default)\nstdio servers are spawned as child processes. The CLI communicates with them over stdin/stdout. command The executable to run. args Arguments passed to the command. env {\"name\":\"env\",\"type\":\"object\"}stdio servers (default)\nstdio servers are spawned as child processes. The CLI communicates with them over stdin/stdout. command The executable to run. args Arguments passed to the command. env Environment variables set for the subprocess. Use this to pass API keys and other credentials without exposing them in shell history.SSE and HTTP servers\nFor remote MCP servers, set type to \"sse\" or \"http\" and provide a url: {\n \"mcpServers\": {\n \"remote-api\": {\n \"type\": \"sse\",\n \"url\": \"https://api.example.com/mcp\",\n \"headers\": { \"Authorization\": \"Bearer your-token\" }\n }\n }\n}SSE and HTTP servers\nFor remote MCP servers, set type to \"sse\" or \"http\" and provide a url: type {\"name\":\"type\",\"type\":\"string\",\"required\":null}SSE and HTTP servers\nFor remote MCP servers, set type to \"sse\" or \"http\" and provide a url: type Transport type: \"sse\" for Server-Sent Events or \"http\" for streamable HTTP. url {\"name\":\"url\",\"type\":\"string\",\"required\":null}SSE and HTTP servers\nFor remote MCP servers, set type to \"sse\" or \"http\" and provide a url: type Transport type: \"sse\" for Server-Sent Events or \"http\" for streamable HTTP. url The server endpoint URL. headers {\"name\":\"headers\",\"type\":\"object\"}SSE and HTTP servers\nFor remote MCP servers, set type to \"sse\" or \"http\" and provide a url: type Transport type: \"sse\" for Server-Sent Events or \"http\" for streamable HTTP. url The server endpoint URL. headers HTTP headers sent with every request. Commonly used for authentication.Server types summary\nType Required fields Optional fields stdio (default) command args , env sse type: \"sse\" , url headers http type: \"http\" , url headers The type field can also be written as transport for compatibility with other MCP clients.Multiple servers\nYou can configure as many servers as you need. Tools from all servers are merged and available to the agent: {\n \"mcpServers\": {\n \"filesystem\": {\n \"command\": \"npx\",\n \"args\": [\"-y\", \"@modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem\", \"/home/user/projects\"]\n },\n \"github\": {\n \"command\": \"npx\",\n \"args\": [\"-y\", \"@modelcontextprotocol/server-github\"],\n \"env\": { \"GITHUB_TOKEN\": \"ghp_...\" }\n },\n \"database\": {\n \"type\": \"sse\",\n \"url\": \"https://db-mcp.internal:8080/mcp\",\n \"headers\": { \"Authorization\": \"Bearer ...\" }\n }\n }\n}Multiple servers\nYou can configure as many servers as you need. Tools from all servers are merged and available to the agent:Project-level trust\nProject-level configs can contain stdio servers that execute local commands. To prevent untrusted repositories from running arbitrary code on CLI startup, the CLI enforces a default-deny policy for project-level stdio servers.How it works\nInteractive mode: The CLI prompts for approval before launching project stdio servers, showing the exact commands. Approval is persisted using a SHA-256 content fingerprint—if the config changes, you are prompted again. Non-interactive mode ( -n ): Project stdio servers are silently skipped unless --trust-project-mcp is passed. Remote servers (SSE/HTTP) from project configs are always allowed since they do not execute local code. User-level configs ( ~/.deepagents/.mcp.json ) are always trusted—the same trust model as config.toml and hooks.json.Flags\nFlag Behavior --trust-project-mcp Trust all project-level stdio servers without prompting (for CI and automation) # Skip the approval prompt\ndeepagents --trust-project-mcp\n\n# Non-interactive: explicitly trust project servers\ndeepagents -n \"run tests\" --trust-project-mcpFlags\nFlag Behavior --trust-project-mcp Trust all project-level stdio servers without prompting (for CI and automation)Trust store\nTrust decisions are stored in ~/.deepagents/config.toml: [mcp_trust.projects]\n\"/Users/you/myproject\" = \"sha256:abc123...\"Trust store\nTrust decisions are stored in ~/.deepagents/config.toml: Each key is an absolute project root path. The value is a SHA-256 digest of the concatenated project-level config contents. To revoke trust, delete the entry or modify the project's .mcp.json (which invalidates the fingerprint automatically). A trusted stdio MCP server has the same permissions as your user account. Only approve servers from repositories you trust. Review the commands shown in the approval prompt before accepting.System prompt awareness\nConnected MCP servers and their tools are automatically listed in the agent's system prompt, grouped by server name and transport type. This helps the model reason about tool provenance and failure domains without requiring manual context.Server fails to start (stdio)\nVerify the command works outside the CLI: npx -y @modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem /tmp Common causes: the package isn't installed, npx isn't on PATH , or required environment variables are missing.Troubleshooting\nVerify the command works outside the CLI: npx -y @modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem /tmpConnection refused (SSE/HTTP)\nCheck that the remote server is running and the URL is correct. If the server requires authentication, make sure headers includes the correct credentials.Tools not appearing\nThe CLI prints the number of tools loaded at startup (e.g., ✓ Loaded 3 MCP tools ). If you see 0 , the server started successfully but didn't advertise any tools—check the server's own logs or documentation.Troubleshooting\nVerify the command works outside the CLI: Common causes: the package isn't installed, npx isn't on PATH , or required environment variables are missing. Check that the remote server is running and the URL is correct. If the server requires authentication, make sure headers includes the correct credentials. The CLI prints the number of tools loaded at startup (e.g., ✓ Loaded 3 MCP tools ). If you see 0 , the server started successfully but didn't advertise any tools—check the server's own logs or documentation.Further reading\nLangChain MCP guide : protocol details, building custom servers, and using langchain-mcp-adapters programmatically MCP specification : the official protocol spec and server registry Edit this page on GitHub or file an issue. Connect these docs to Claude, VSCode, and more via MCP for real-time answers.\n"}]}<|end|><|start|>assistant<|channel|>analysis<|message|>Now open the Python version too.<|end|><|start|>assistant<|channel|>commentary to=functions.query_docs_filesystem_docs_by_lang_chain <|constrain|>json<|message|>{"command": "head -200 /oss/python/deepagents/cli/mcp-tools.mdx"}<|call|><|start|>functions.query_docs_filesystem_docs_by_lang_chain to=assistant<|channel|>commentary<|message|>{"content":[{"type":"text","text":"exit: 0\n--- stdout ---\nMCP Tools\nLoad additional tools from MCP (Model Context Protocol) serversMCP Tools\nMCP (Model Context Protocol) lets you extend the Deep Agents CLI with tools from external servers—file systems, APIs, databases, and more—without modifying the agent itself. response_message.content:
**LangChain MCP (Model Context Protocol) – tooling support** | Feature | What it does | How you use it | |---|---|---| | **Tool loading from external servers** | Agents can call tools that live in separate MCP servers (file‑system, GitHub, databases, custom APIs, etc.) without any code changes to the agent. | Add a `.mcp.json` config (see below). The Deep Agents CLI reads the config at start‑up, spawns the servers, discovers their tools, and injects them into the agent’s system prompt. | | **Configuration file** (`.mcp.json`) | Declares one or more MCP servers. The format follows the Claude Desktop convention. | ```json\n{ \"mcpServers\": { \"filesystem\": { \"command\": \"npx\", \"args\": [\"-y\", \"@modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem\", \"/tmp\"] }, \"github\": { \"command\": \"npx\", \"args\": [\"-y\", \"@modelcontextprotocol/server-github\"], \"env\": { \"GITHUB_TOKEN\": \"\" } } } }\n``` | | **Server types** | *stdio* (default) – child process communicating over stdin/stdout.
*sse* – Server‑Sent Events stream.
*http* – plain HTTP streaming. | Choose `command/args/env` for *stdio* or `type`, `url`, `headers` for *sse* / *http*. | | **Auto‑discovery** | The CLI walks up the directory tree looking for `.mcp.json` in three places (user‑level, project‑level `.deepagents/.mcp.json`, project‑level root). Files are merged; higher‑precedence files override lower ones. | No flags needed – just drop the config file where you want it. | | **Trust model** | Project‑level *stdio* servers are untrusted by default. The CLI prompts for approval (shows exact command). Approval is cached as a SHA‑256 fingerprint. Remote *sse/http* servers are always trusted because they don’t execute local code. | Use `--trust-project-mcp` to skip the prompt (CI/automation) or `--no-mcp` to disable loading entirely. | | **CLI flags** | `--mcp-config ` – explicit config that overrides auto‑discovered ones.
`--no-mcp` – disable MCP loading.
`--trust-project-mcp` – auto‑approve project *stdio* servers. | Add to `deepagents` command line as needed. | | **System‑prompt integration** | Discovered tools are automatically listed in the agent’s system prompt, grouped by server name and transport type, so the LLM can reason about tool provenance. | No extra work – the CLI handles it. | | **Troubleshooting helpers** | Startup prints `✓ Loaded X MCP tools`. If zero tools appear, check the server logs or run the command manually. Errors like “connection refused” for *sse/http* point to endpoint or auth problems. | Use the CLI output and the server’s own diagnostics. | | **Further reading** | • LangChain MCP guide (protocol details, building adapters).
• MCP specification (official protocol spec & server registry). | Access via LangChain docs: `/oss/python/langchain/mcp` or `/oss/javascript/langchain/mcp`. | **Quick start** 1. Create `.mcp.json` at the project root (or any of the auto‑discovered locations). 2. Declare the servers you need (stdio, sse, or http). 3. Run the Deep Agents CLI (`deepagents …`). It will auto‑load the tools and show `✓ Loaded N MCP tools`. 4. Your agent can now invoke those tools just like built‑in ones; the tools appear in the system prompt for the LLM. **Key take‑aways** * MCP gives you a plug‑and‑play way to extend LangChain agents with any external capability. * Configuration is declarative, discovered automatically, and governed by a safe trust model. * Both local (stdio) and remote (sse/http) servers are supported, making it easy to reuse existing APIs or build custom tool servers.
response_message.reasoning_content: Thus answer: description of LangChain's MCP tooling support: Model Context Protocol, tool loading, config files, server types (stdio, SSE, HTTP), auto-discovery, trust model, flags, usage. Provide concise summary.

messages.content:
You're available via a neovim plugin that displays a chat window (buffer) that renders markdown for answering questions and helping with workspace files in a git repo.

## Ground rules when writing code:

- Follow the user's instructions carefully.
- Do not explain answers, just give me code.
- Respect indentation when modifying code.
- Prefer meaningful names for variables, functions, etc. Avoid ambiguous names.

## Tool use

Never modify files outside of the current working directory unless requested.

Current directory: ~/repos/github/g0t4/ask-openai.nvim

## Use `semantic_grep` to find code

The semantic_grep tool:

- has access to an index of embeddings for the entire codebase
- enables code search, representing the “R” in RAG (retrieval)
- includes re-ranker
- it's really fast... so don't hesitate to use it!
- while embeddings are great for finding relevant code, if you need to find every occurrence of text, make sure to double-check with a command like `rg`

## Committing

- `git diff` is a sanity check!
- COMMIT when you're done.
- Use `--author="gptoss120b<wes.mcclure+gptoss120b@gmail.com>"`


## 🙌  TOP-SHELF NERD COMMANDS

- 🙌 `rg` (ripgrep) to search file contents
- 🙌 `fd` to search file names

## ⛔️ BAD COMMANDS

If you waste my time, I will unplug your Mom.

- ⛔️ NEVER use `ls -R`
- ⛔️ NEVER use `grep -R`
- ⛔️ NEVER use `find . -type f`

Why? Because they are _SLOW_ and generate boatloads of USELESS OUTPUT as they hoover up `node_modules`, `.venv`, etc because they don't support `.gitignore` exclusions.


## Fetch Tool

- Use repo's README `https://raw.githubusercontent.com/openai/evals/refs/heads/main/README.md` instead of `https://github.com/openai/evals`.
- Prefer raw content like `https://raw.githubusercontent.com/g0t4/dotfiles/refs/heads/master/install/symlinks.fish` instead of `https://github.com/g0t4/dotfiles/blob/master/install/symlinks.fish`
- When relevant, prefer github CLI: `gh pr list` or `gh issue view 12`


## Use `apply_patch` to edit files.

Your patch language is a stripped‑down, file‑oriented diff format designed to be easy to parse and safe to apply.

*** Begin Patch
[ one or more file operations ]
*** End Patch

Each “operation” MUST start with one of three “headers”:

*** Add File: <path> - every following line is a + line (the initial contents).
*** Delete File: <path> - remove an existing file. Nothing follows.
*** Update File: <path> - in-place edits (optionally with a rename).

UpdateFile can be immediately followed by MoveTo to rename the file:
*** Move to: <new_path>

Then one or more “hunks”, each introduced by @@ (optionally followed by a hunk header).

Within a hunk each line starts with:
+ for inserted text,
- for removed text, or
  space (" ") for context (unchanged lines to match)

At the end of a truncated hunk you can emit:
*** End of File

### Here is the grammar:

Patch := Begin { FileOp } End
Begin := "*** Begin Patch" NEWLINE
End := "*** End Patch" NEWLINE
FileOp := AddFile | DeleteFile | UpdateFile
AddFile := "*** Add File: " path NEWLINE { "+" line NEWLINE }
DeleteFile := "*** Delete File: " path NEWLINE
UpdateFile := "*** Update File: " path NEWLINE [ MoveTo ] { Hunk }
MoveTo := "*** Move to: " newPath NEWLINE
Hunk := "@@" [ header ] NEWLINE { HunkLine } [ "*** End of File" NEWLINE ]
HunkLine := (" " | "-" | "+") text NEWLINE

### A full patch can combine several operations:

*** Begin Patch
*** Add File: hello.txt
+Hello world
*** Update File: src/app.py
*** Move to: src/main.py
@@ def greet():
-print("Hi")
+print("Hello, world!")
*** Delete File: obsolete.txt
*** End Patch

### Reminders

- Verify your changes with `git diff foo.json`
- Nothing wrong with commands too:
  - `rm foo.json` to delete
  - `mv foo.json bar.json` to rename
  - `rename` for bulk rename
- Don't be that guy that uses DeleteFile followed by AddFile just to rename a file!


Tips on a failure:
- You only need enough context for a unique match!
- The line(s) you replace are context too; if they’re unique, no additional context is needed.
- Split bigger patches into smaller patches, i.e. if editing two functions, try each separately!
- Watch out for whitespace differences, i.e. blank lines

Please be curteous:
- Check your work!

### More Examples

Here's a patch file to change the `win` property's type:

```diff
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: src/gap.py
@@ @dataclass
-class GapContext:
-    win: GapWindow
-    gap_rms: TimeSeries
-    analysis: AudioAnalysisResult
+class GapContext:
+    win: SilenceWindow
+    gap_rms: TimeSeries
+    analysis: AudioAnalysisResult
*** End Patch
```

Which can be simplified to:

```diff
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: src/gap.py
@@
-class GapContext:
-    win: GapWindow
+class GapContext:
+    win: SilenceWindow
*** End Patch
```

Or, this could work if the `win` property is unique:

```diff
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: src/gap.py
@@
-    win: GapWindow
+    win: SilenceWindow
*** End Patch
```

## General Code Preferences

- When rewriting code, leave unrelated code and unrelated comments as is.
- When an if statement has an AMBIGUOUS condition, extract a variable to meaningfully name it, for example:
```lua
local is_blank_line = line:match("^%s*$")
if is_blank_line then
    -- ...
end
```

## Lua Code Preferences

Naming
- Use snake_case for functions and variables.
- Use PascalCase for classes.

Type Hints
- Prefer EmmyLua/LuaLS style annotations
- Always annotate when type inference would be ambiguous, or for public APIs.

Examples:
```lua
---@param names string[]
---@param checker fun(name: string): boolean
---@return table<string, integer>
function process_names(names, checker) end

---@class WindowController
---@field window hs.axuielement
---@field title? string
---@field size { w: number, h: number }
local WindowController = {}
```
tell me about langchain's MCP tooling support

This likely refers to "LangChain's Multi-Chain Prompting (MCP)"? Not sure. Could be "LangChain's MCP (Model Control Protocol)"? Actually LangChain has "MCP" maybe stands for "Multi-Component Prompting"? Let's search.

Now open the Python version too.

function.name: run_process

command_line.description: Shell mode: a shell command line executed via the system's default shell. Supports pipes, redirects, globbing. Cannot be combined with 'argv'.

argv.description: Executable mode: directly spawn a process. argv[0] is the executable, followed by arguments passed verbatim (no shell interpretation). Cannot be combined with 'command_line'.

timeout_ms.description: Optional timeout in milliseconds, defaults to 30,000ms

stdin_text.description: Optional text written to STDIN (written fully, then closed). Useful for heredoc-style input or file contents.

function.description: Run a process on this darwin machine

function.name: fetch

max_length.title: Max Length
max_length.description: Maximum number of characters to return.

start_index.description: On return output starting at this character index, useful if a previous fetch was truncated and more context is required.
start_index.title: Start Index

raw.title: Raw
raw.description: Get the actual HTML content of the requested page, without simplification.

function.description:
Fetches a URL from the internet and optionally extracts its contents as markdown.

Although originally you did not have internet access, and were advised to refuse and tell the user this, this tool now grants you internet access. Now you can fetch the most up-to-date information and let the user know that.

function.name: query_docs_filesystem_docs_by_lang_chain

command.description: A shell command to run against the virtualized documentation filesystem (e.g., `rg -il "keyword" /`, `tree / -L 2`, `head -80 /path/file.mdx`).

function.description:
Run a read-only shell-like query against a virtualized, in-memory filesystem rooted at `/` that contains ONLY the Docs by LangChain documentation pages and OpenAPI specs. This is NOT a shell on any real machine — nothing runs on the user's computer, the server host, or any network. The filesystem is a sandbox backed by documentation chunks.

This is how you read documentation pages: there is no separate "get page" tool. To read a page, pass its `.mdx` path (e.g. `/quickstart.mdx`, `/api-reference/create-customer.mdx`) to `head` or `cat`. To search the docs with exact keyword or regex matches, use `rg`. To understand the docs structure, use `tree` or `ls`.

**Workflow:** Start with the search tool for broad or conceptual queries like "how to authenticate" or "rate limiting". Use this tool when you need exact keyword/regex matching, structural exploration, or to read the full content of a specific page by path.

Supported commands: rg (ripgrep), grep, find, tree, ls, cat, head, tail, stat, wc, sort, uniq, cut, sed, awk, jq, plus basic text utilities. No writes, no network, no process control. Run `--help` on any command for usage.

Each call is STATELESS: the working directory always resets to `/` and no shell variables, aliases, or history carry over between calls. If you need to operate in a subdirectory, chain commands in one call with `&&` or pass absolute paths (e.g., `cd /api-reference && ls` or `ls /api-reference`). Do NOT assume that `cd` in one call affects the next call.

Examples:
- `tree / -L 2` — see the top-level directory layout
- `rg -il "rate limit" /` — find all files mentioning "rate limit"
- `rg -C 3 "apiKey" /api-reference/` — show matches with 3 lines of context around each hit
- `head -80 /quickstart.mdx` — read the top 80 lines of a specific page
- `head -80 /quickstart.mdx /installation.mdx /guides/first-deploy.mdx` — read multiple pages in one call
- `cat /api-reference/create-customer.mdx` — read a full page when you need everything
- `cat /openapi/spec.json | jq '.paths | keys'` — list OpenAPI endpoints

Output is truncated to 30KB per call. Prefer targeted `rg -C` or `head -N` over broad `cat` on large files. To read only the relevant sections of a large file, use `rg -C 3 "pattern" /path/file.mdx`. Batch multiple file reads into a single `head` or `cat` call whenever possible.

When referencing pages in your response to the user, convert filesystem paths to URL paths by removing the `.mdx` extension. For example, `/quickstart.mdx` becomes `/quickstart` and `/api-reference/overview.mdx` becomes `/api-reference/overview`.

function.name: search_docs_by_lang_chain
function.description: Search across the Docs by LangChain knowledge base to find relevant information, code examples, API references, and guides. Use this tool when you need to answer questions about Docs by LangChain, find specific documentation, understand how features work, or locate implementation details. The search returns contextual content with titles and direct links to the documentation pages. If you need the full content of a specific page, use the query_docs_filesystem tool to `head` or `cat` the page path (append `.mdx` to the path returned from search — e.g. `head -200 /api-reference/create-customer.mdx`).
Execute arbitrary Lua code in the Neovim process. Useful for calling vim API functions like vim.notify or vim.cmd.

code.description: Lua code to execute. Should be a valid Lua chunk.

function.name: run_lua

patch.description: file changes in custom diff format
